nativeRange.habitat: Cultivar derived from Bucephalandra populations endemic to Borneo, where the parent species grow as rheophytes attached to rocks and submerged wood along fast-flowing rainforest streams and rivers

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to
good flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; suitable for foreground or midground placement
stable, established tank — do not introduce into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: The Bucephalandra Brownie is a compact cultivar from Borneo with dark brown-green, wavy-edged leaves that flash purple and blue iridescence under moderate light. Like all Bucephalandra, it grows attached to hardscape rather than planted in substrate, and the rhizome must never be buried.
careNotes: The Bucephalandra Brownie is a long-standing hobby cultivar endemic to Borneo, characterised by relatively compact, wavy-edged leaves that present a dark brown-to-deep-green tone, with reported purple and blue iridescent flashes under optimal lighting. The Brownie group is broad in the trade — multiple sub-cultivars are sold under the Brownie umbrella (including 'Brownie Phantom', 'Brownie Blue', 'Brownie Ghost', and 'Brownie Phoenix'); this entry covers the baseline Brownie type form. The creeping rhizome is attached to lava rock, driftwood, or slate using super glue gel, cotton thread, or fishing line, and must not be buried in substrate or it is generally reported to rot. Lighting is flexible: low to moderate light is sufficient, with the characteristic Brownie iridescence emerging more strongly under medium light. CO2 supplementation is optional but generally considered to enhance color and growth rate. Growth is very slow — commonly reported as one new leaf every two to four weeks — and adult size typically stays in the compact 5–10 cm range. As with most imported Bucephalandra, newly arrived plants tend to undergo a 'buce melt' as emersed-grown leaves are shed and submersed-form leaves grow in from the rhizome; as long as the rhizome stays firm, recovery is generally reported over several weeks. Introduction into an established, stable aquarium is widely advised.
breedingNotes: The Bucephalandra Brownie is propagated by rhizome division. Once established, cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots — at least roughly 4 cm of rhizome per division is generally recommended. Reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sub-cultivars in the Brownie group are maintained vegetatively rather than by seed in hobby setups.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (70-82°F / 21-28°C), lighting (low to moderate), CO2 (recommended for color/growth), placement (foreground to midground), propagation by rhizome division producing side shoots, attachment to hardscape with no rhizome burial, and coloration description (dark green/brown with purple/blue iridescence).
